headline: Parliamentary opposition has zero formal recognition in Standing Orders
context: Malaysia's parliamentary Standing Orders contain no formal recognition of the Leader of the Opposition, opposition rights, or shadow cabinet — unlike most Westminster systems.

cards.big: Opposition leader has no formal status in parliamentary rules — unique among Westminster systems
cards.sub: Standing Orders do not mention opposition, shadow cabinet, or minority rights

cards.big: Standing Orders were last comprehensively revised in 1959 — 67 years ago
cards.sub: Colonial-era parliamentary rules govern a multi-party modern democracy

cards.big: Opposition leader receives no official budget, staff, or resources for scrutiny functions
cards.sub: Effective opposition requires resources — Malaysia provides essentially none

cards.big: UK Parliament Act 1975 formally provides resources and recognition for opposition
cards.sub: Westminster's own Parliament recognizes opposition — Malaysia's copy does not

cards.big: A parliament that doesn't formally recognize opposition doesn't formally recognize dissent
Should Standing Orders be reformed to formally recognise and resource the opposition?
